message.content:
## Web Searcher: trademark verification — USPTO/EUIPO class 9 & 42 software marks

Research question: "Find the best available brand name for a software product, and verify availability for each finalist. 

THE PRODUCT: an autonomous "deep research" AI agent/pipeline. It fans out web + academic searches across many sources, then writes a long-form research report (e.g. literature reviews, market/industry analyses) in which EVERY claim is traced back to a real, verifiable citation. Its core differentiator vs competitors is FAITHFULNESS and RIGOR — a built-in verifier repairs/re-grounds each sentence to source evidence so the report does not hallucinate. Think of it as "a research analyst that never makes up a fact." Positioning themes to draw from: truth / veracity / faithfulness / proof, depth / synthesis / research, insight / clarity / illumination, and (secondarily) autonomous agentic speed.

CURRENT NAME TO REPLACE: "Polaris" (rejected — too generic, and heavily trademarked: Polaris Inc. snowmobiles, Polaris missiles, many software products). AVOID this and the whole cliché family of star/compass/navigation/"north star" names.

NAMING STYLE: open — prefer short (2-3 syllables), pronounceable, brandable names. Coined/abstract words (like Vercel, Cohere, Anthropic, Kadoa) are welcome because they are the most ownable; obscure real words or Greek/Latin/other-language roots with a truth/insight meaning are also welcome. Avoid the crowded, already-taken names in the AI-research space — explicitly check and EXCLUDE names already used by: Elicit, Consensus, Perplexity, Undermind, Scite, You.com, Genspark, Storm/STORM (Stanford), Ai2/OpenScholar, Scholarcy, Semantic Scholar, Deep Research (OpenAI/Google/Perplexity feature names), Glean, Hebbia, Vespa, and any obvious near-duplicates.

DELIVERABLE: a RANKED shortlist of 8-12 candidate names. For EACH candidate provide:
  1. The name, its pronunciation, and a 1-2 sentence meaning/rationale tying it to the truth/rigor/deep-research positioning.
  2. GitHub availability: is the org/username taken? (check github.com/<name>).
  3. Trademark risk: search USPTO TESS and EUIPO for live marks in the software/AI classes (Nice class 9 and 42); flag any conflict, note if clear.
  4. Domain availability: is <name>.ai and/or <name>.com registered/parked/available? Note approximate acquisition feasibility.
  5. Social handle: is @<name> (or a close variant) free on X/Twitter?
  6. A risk flag: GREEN (all clear), YELLOW (minor/partial conflict), or RED (real conflict) with one-line reason.
Then give a clear TOP RECOMMENDATION (1-2 names) that best balances meaning + full availability across all four channels, and explain why. Prioritize candidates that are GREEN on ALL of: GitHub org, trademark, at least one of .ai/.com, and a social handle. It is better to return fewer names that are genuinely fully-available than many that are taken."

Your angle: **trademark verification — USPTO/EUIPO class 9 & 42 software marks** — Establishes the method and sources for checking trademark risk on each finalist, which is a required deliverable field and a hard GREEN/RED gate.
Search query: `how to search USPTO TESS EUIPO live trademark software Nice class 9 42 clearance name availability`

## Task
Use WebSearch with the query above (or a refined version). Return the top 4-6 most relevant results.
Rank by relevance to the ORIGINAL question, not just the search query. Skip obvious SEO spam/content farms.
Include a short snippet capturing why each result is relevant.

Structured output only.

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- artifact-design: Design guidance and fundamentals for Artifacts.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a GitHub pull request; for your working diff use /code-review
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

Links: [{"title":"Trademark Search: How To Search USPTO Trademark Database | PatentPC","url":"https://patentpc.com/blog/trademark-search-how-to-search-uspto-trademark-database"},{"title":"International Trademark Classes - Corsearch","url":"https://corsearch.com/content-library/blog/international-trademark-classes/"},{"title":"TESS Is Gone: How to Search Trademarks in 2026 | Signa Blog","url":"https://signa.so/blog/tess-is-gone-how-to-search-trademarks"},{"title":"Trademark Search 🔎 Free Tool","url":"https://trademark-search.marcaria.com/en"},{"title":"Trademark Class Search","url":"https://trademark-search.marcaria.com/en/trademark-class-search"},{"title":"Nice Agreement current edition version - general remarks, class headings and explanatory notes | USPTO","url":"https://www.uspto.gov/trademarks/trademark-updates-and-announcements/nice-agreement-current-edition-version-general-remarks"},{"title":"Searching the Trademark ID Manual | USPTO","url":"https://www.uspto.gov/trademarks/guides-and-manuals/searching-trademark-id-manual"}]

Based on the search results, here's information about searching for trademarks in USPTO TESS, EUIPO, and related databases for Nice classes 9 and 42:

## Key Information:

**Nice Classes 9 and 42:**
A tech company developing software would focus on Class 9 (software) and Class 42 (software services). Class 42 covers software as a service (SaaS).

**Searching by Class:**
Using the TESS interface, you can refine your search to include all relevant classes that pertain to your business. All faceted filters (offices, Nice classes, dates, status) live nested under filters in search systems.

**Important Note about TESS:**
TESS only covered US trademarks, and the new USPTO system has the same scope.

**International Trademark Searching:**
For broader clearance work beyond the US, the World Intellectual Property Organization's Global Brand Database is the broadest free trademark search tool available and covers over 70 million records from 73 national and regional trademark offices. Additionally, TMview is managed by EUIPO and covers over 75 million trademarks from EU member states.

**Nice Classification Reference:**
The Nice Classification is used to determine appropriate classification of goods and services in trademark applications filed on or after September 1, 1973.


REMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks.

content.text: One critical finding is that TESS has been retired. Let me verify the current USPTO search tool and confirm details on the EUIPO/WIPO tools.

input.query: USPTO trademark search system replaced TESS 2026 new tool EUIPO eSearch WIPO Global Brand Database how to check live marks

content.content:
Web search results for query: "USPTO trademark search system replaced TESS 2026 new tool EUIPO eSearch WIPO Global Brand Database how to check live marks"

Links: [{"title":"TESS Is Gone: How to Search Trademarks in 2026 | Signa Blog","url":"https://signa.so/blog/tess-is-gone-how-to-search-trademarks"},{"title":"Transitioning from TESS to new search | USPTO","url":"https://www.uspto.gov/about-us/events/transitioning-tess-new-search"},{"title":"Trademark search system updates | USPTO","url":"https://www.uspto.gov/trademarks/search/trademark-search-system-updates"},{"title":"Retiring TESS: What to know about the new trademark search system | USPTO","url":"https://www.uspto.gov/subscription-center/2023/retiring-tess-what-know-about-new-trademark-search-system"},{"title":"Search our trademark database | USPTO","url":"https://www.uspto.gov/trademarks/search"},{"title":"About the USPTO’s New Trademark Search System - Alt Legal","url":"https://www.altlegal.com/blog/about-the-usptos-new-trademark-search-system/"},{"title":"What to expect from our new trademark search system (and why we’re replacing TESS) | USPTO","url":"https://www.uspto.gov/subscription-center/2023/what-expect-our-new-trademark-search-system-and-why-were-replacing-tess"},{"title":"DATABASES - Trademarks - Research Guides at Rutgers University","url":"https://libguides.rutgers.edu/trademarks/databases"},{"title":"TESS Is Gone: The New USPTO Trademark Search (What Changed)","url":"https://gleanmark.com/insights/tess-vs-gleanmark-trademark-search-comparison"},{"title":"A Crash Course in TESS II, the USPTO’s New Trademark Search System (Godspeed, TESS) - BOAG LAW","url":"https://www.boagip.com/a-crash-course-in-tess-ii-the-usptos-new-trademark-search-system-godspeed-tess/"}]

Based on the search results, here's what I found about the USPTO trademark search system replacement and international alternatives:

## USPTO's TESS Replacement

The USPTO replaced TESS with a cloud-based system called Trademark Search, accessible at tmsearch.uspto.gov, with the transition happening on November 30, 2023. Old TESS URLs now redirect to the new system.

### Key Features of the New System

The new system uses industry-standard search syntax with Boolean operators (AND, OR, NOT), field tags for targeting specific data fields, truncation operators, and proximity searching, with a Search Builder tool to help construct complex queries. The details page shows most information from TSDR without having to leave the search system.

### Limitations

The USPTO database contains only US trademark filings, so if you need to check whether a mark is registered in the EU, Canada, Singapore, or anywhere else, you need a different tool. The new system has no public API, though the USPTO offers bulk data downloads through the Open Data Portal.

## International Trademark Search Tools

For international coverage, the World Intellectual Property Organization's Global Brand Database is the broadest free trademark search tool available. The Global Brand Database allows you to perform a trademark search by text or image in brand data from multiple national and international sources, including trademarks, appellations of origin and official emblems.


REMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks.

content.text: I have strong, well-sourced results. The key correction is that TESS is retired and replaced by tmsearch.uspto.gov. Let me compile the ranked findings.

results.title: Search our trademark database | USPTO (Trademark Search — the TESS replacement)
results.snippet: The authoritative, free US-side gate for every finalist. TESS was retired Nov 30, 2023 and replaced by the cloud-based Trademark Search at tmsearch.uspto.gov — old TESS URLs now redirect here. This is where you run the required Class 9 (software) and Class 42 (SaaS/software services) clearance for each candidate name and confirm whether any conflicting mark is LIVE vs dead.

results.title: A Crash Course in TESS II, the USPTO's New Trademark Search System — BOAG LAW
results.snippet: Practical how-to for the new USPTO system: Boolean operators (AND/OR/NOT), field tags, truncation, and proximity searching, plus the Search Builder. Directly enables the method needed to search a candidate name string, filter to LIVE marks, and restrict to Nice classes 9 and 42 — the exact query construction required to produce the GREEN/YELLOW/RED trademark verdict per name.

results.title: TESS Is Gone: How to Search Trademarks in 2026 | Signa Blog
results.snippet: Current (2026) walkthrough confirming TESS's retirement and how to search today. Notes that the USPTO system covers US filings only, so EU clearance needs a separate tool — the key caveat for the deliverable, which requires both USPTO and EUIPO checks. Points to WIPO Global Brand Database and EUIPO's TMview for multi-jurisdiction coverage.

results.title: International Trademark Classes — Corsearch
results.snippet: Explains the Nice Classification and confirms software products live in Class 9 (downloadable software) with Class 42 covering software-as-a-service. Establishes exactly which two classes to filter on when clearing an AI-software brand name, the load-bearing scoping decision for the trademark field of each candidate.

results.title: Searching the Trademark ID Manual | USPTO
results.snippet: The USPTO ID Manual lets you confirm the correct goods/services descriptions and class assignments (9 vs 42) for AI/deep-research software before searching, ensuring the clearance query targets the right Nice classes rather than missing conflicts filed under adjacent identifications.

results.title: WIPO Global Brand Database (broadest free multi-office search)
results.snippet: The broadest free trademark search tool — 70M+ records across 70+ national/regional offices, including EUIPO — enabling the EU-side and international leg of the required per-name clearance that the US-only tmsearch.uspto.gov cannot cover. Pair with EUIPO's TMview for EU-specific live-mark checks in classes 9 and 42.
